narration_segments.text: There is a moment — and every grieving person knows it — when the body becomes heavier than it has ever been. It happens in the morning. The eyes open. For one half-second, the world is normal. Then memory arrives. And with memory comes weight. A stone drops into the chest. The lungs tighten. The arms feel pinned to the mattress. The jaw locks. Even the skin feels thick, as though the body has been wrapped in wet cloth overnight. This is not metaphor. This is physiology. This is what grief does to a living body.

Ming stands at the window of her apartment on the fourteenth floor. Below, the city moves. Cars. Buses. People crossing at the light. All of it continuing, indifferent, unstoppable. Seven weeks ago, her mother died. Ming expected sadness. She expected tears. She expected the hollow ache that everyone warned her about. But she did not expect this — this numbness in her hands. This feeling that her body belongs to someone else. This strange sensation that she is watching her own life from behind thick glass, present but not participating, alive but not living. She eats because the clock says noon. She sleeps because the clock says eleven. She answers emails because the notification sound triggers a reflex that has nothing to do with desire or intention. Her body moves through routines her mind has abandoned. At the funeral, her brother wept openly. Her aunt collapsed against the casket. Ming stood dry-eyed and still. Later, in the car, her cousin said, "You are so strong." Ming said nothing. She did not feel strong. She felt frozen. As though someone had poured cement into the center of her chest and it had hardened before she could breathe it out.

The practice begins with the hands. Place both palms over the center of the chest. Not pressing hard. Not hovering. Resting. Let the warmth of the hands seep through the skin, through the muscle, through the bone, into the space where the heart beats. Feel the contact. Feel the weight. Feel the simple, animal comfort of touch. This is the first act of the practice: presence. Not fixing. Not healing. Not even intending to heal. Simply arriving. Telling the heart: I am here. I am not leaving. I am paying attention. Now notice what the heart offers back. It might be tightness. It might be heat. It might be a dull ache. It might be nothing at all — just the steady thump of a muscle doing its work. Whatever comes, let it come. Do not judge it. Do not interpret it. Do not assign it meaning.

There is a paradox at the center of grief that no one explains in advance: the way through is not around. Every instinct says avoid. Every reflex says protect. The body armors the chest. The mind builds walls of distraction — work, noise, scrolling, busyness, anything to keep the stillness at bay. Because in the stillness, the absence is loudest. In the stillness, the chair is empty. The phone does not ring. The name is never called from the other room. And yet.
